This article is about the song by Coldplay. For the album, see [*Viva la Vida or Death and All His Friends*](/wiki/Viva_la_Vida_or_Death_and_All_His_Friends "Viva la Vida or Death and All His Friends"). "**Viva la Vida**" ([/ˌviːvə lə ˈviːdə/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*VEE-və lə VEE-də*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key"), Spanish: [[ˈbiβa la ˈβiða]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Spanish "Help:IPA/Spanish"); Spanish for 'long live life')[[4]](#cite_note-:8-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5) is a song by British [rock](/wiki/Rock_music "Rock music") band [Coldplay](/wiki/Coldplay "Coldplay"). It was written by all members of the band for their fourth album, *[Viva la Vida or Death and All His Friends](/wiki/Viva_la_Vida_or_Death_and_All_His_Friends "Viva la Vida or Death and All His Friends")* (2008), on which the band explored new styles. The lyrics contain historical and Christian references (King [Louis XVI](/wiki/Louis_XVI "Louis XVI") and the [French Revolution](/wiki/French_Revolution "French Revolution"), "Roman cavalry choirs", [Saint Peter](/wiki/Saint_Peter "Saint Peter")) and the track is built around a looping [string section](/wiki/String_section "String section") with a digitally processed piano, while other layers are gradually added.

The title was taken from Mexican artist [Frida Kahlo](/wiki/Frida_Kahlo "Frida Kahlo")'s final painting in which the words "Viva la Vida" were inscribed on a [watermelon](/wiki/Watermelon "Watermelon"). [Chris Martin](/wiki/Chris_Martin "Chris Martin") had seen the painting while at the [Frida Kahlo museum](/wiki/Frida_Kahlo_Museum "Frida Kahlo Museum") in [Mexico](/wiki/Mexico "Mexico"), and thought it was a good title for a song. The song primarily tells the story of a king who has lost his kingdom, and reflects on the social aspects of [revolution](/wiki/Revolution "Revolution"). The song went through several different styles as the band struggled to settle on its composition. Eventually, a church bell sound effect was added which completed the sound they were searching for. After hearing the bell, Martin remarked: "That's it."

The track was first made available as part of the album pre-order on 7 May 2008, being released individually on 25 May 2008 as the album's second single. Debuting to critical acclaim and commercial success, "Viva la Vida" reached the top spot of the [UK Singles Chart](/wiki/UK_Singles_Chart "UK Singles Chart") and [*Billboard* Hot 100](/wiki/Billboard_Hot_100 "Billboard Hot 100"), becoming the band's first number-one single in both the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States") and [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom"). It won the [Grammy Award for Song of the Year](/wiki/Grammy_Award_for_Song_of_the_Year "Grammy Award for Song of the Year") at the [51st Annual Grammy Awards](/wiki/51st_Annual_Grammy_Awards "51st Annual Grammy Awards") in 2009 and has been featured on several critic rankings and listicles. It is one of the most-streamed songs on [Spotify](/wiki/Spotify "Spotify") with over 3.1 billion plays.

Two music videos were produced for the song. The official version, directed by [Hype Williams](/wiki/Hype_Williams "Hype Williams"), depicts the band performing against a blurry, warped version of [Eugène Delacroix](/wiki/Eug%C3%A8ne_Delacroix "Eugène Delacroix")'s *[Liberty Leading the People](/wiki/Liberty_Leading_the_People "Liberty Leading the People")*. A second, alternate video is a tribute to director [Anton Corbijn](/wiki/Anton_Corbijn "Anton Corbijn")'s video for [Depeche Mode](/wiki/Depeche_Mode "Depeche Mode")'s "[Enjoy the Silence](/wiki/Enjoy_the_Silence "Enjoy the Silence")" (1990) and portrays Martin as the king from whose perspective the song is sung.

Multiple artists claimed that the band had [plagiarised](/wiki/Plagiarism "Plagiarism") parts of their work to produce the song. American band Creaky Boards suggested Coldplay had taken from their song "The Songs I didn't Write", though this was later retracted. [Joe Satriani](/wiki/Joe_Satriani "Joe Satriani") took the band to court over alleged similarities to song "If I Could Fly" (2004), but the case was dismissed. [Cat Stevens](/wiki/Cat_Stevens "Cat Stevens") claimed the song was similar to his 1973 song "[Foreigner Suite](/wiki/Foreigner_(Cat_Stevens_album) "Foreigner (Cat Stevens album)")", but also said that he didn't think they copied it on purpose.

## Background and writing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Viva_la_Vida&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Background and writing")]

The song's Spanish title is taken from a painting by 20th-century [Mexican](/wiki/Mexico "Mexico") artist [Frida Kahlo](/wiki/Frida_Kahlo "Frida Kahlo"). In what was her final painting, which was finalised a few days before her death, the words "Viva la Vida" are inscribed on a [melon](/wiki/Watermelon "Watermelon") wedge at the bottom of the canvas.[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-:2-7) In Spanish, *viva* translates to "long live",[[8]](#cite_note-Diccionario_de_la_lengua_Española-8) so "Long Live Life" is an accurate translation for the title. The painting reflects the artistic irony of acclaiming life while suffering physically.[[9]](#cite_note-Frida-Kahlo-9) When asked about the album's title, referring to Frida Kahlo's strength, enduring [polio](/wiki/Polio "Polio"), a broken spine, and a decade of chronic pain, lead singer [Chris Martin](/wiki/Chris_Martin "Chris Martin") said: "She went through a lot of shit, of course, and then she started a big painting in her house that said 'Viva la Vida', I just loved the boldness of it."[[10]](#cite_note-rollingstone-10)

In a 2011 interview for [Sveriges Television](/wiki/Sveriges_Television "Sveriges Television")'s documentary TV series "Hitlåtens historia" (Swedish for 'history of the hit song'), Martin recalled: "This is how it started—we were in Mexico and we were at the Frida Kahlo museum, and she has a painting called Viva la Vida, and I thought 'Oh that's a cool title—I'd like to use that' and I have a list of titles that I like, and so it was in my head to try and write a song called Viva la Vida." Martin continued: "And then a little big of melody came to me about 2 o'clock in the morning, so I went downstairs to the piano ... and just ... then that chord sequence came out ... then it all just came out in about 10 minutes ... and then the next year was spent trying to record it ... so it was a really, crazy, fast arrival, and then a very long process to finish it."[[11]](#cite_note-:7-11)

In another interview, Martin explained he thought of the "I used to rule the world" lyric after he had taken sleeping pills: "I thought, 'God, that sounds like a big hit single to me,' and then the other half of me said, 'Yeah, you should go to bed.' And then the other half replied, 'No, no, go downstairs and work it out.' So I went down to find a guitar and I recorded it, and I'm very glad because it's an important song for us."[[12]](#cite_note-12) In a 2016 interview on [The Howard Stern Show](/wiki/The_Howard_Stern_Show "The Howard Stern Show"), Martin explained: "That just came out in, sort of, one go ... There was probably eight other songs that I tried to call Viva la Vida that never made it ... but then the real one came through and that was the one"[[7]](#cite_note-:2-7)[[13]](#cite_note-13)

## Production and composition

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Viva_la_Vida&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Production and composition")]

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/87/Gnome-mime-sound-openclipart.svg/60px-Gnome-mime-sound-openclipart.svg.png)

["Viva la Vida"](/wiki/File:Viva_la_Vida.ogg "File:Viva la Vida.ogg")

The sample contains the beginning snippet of the song

---

*Problems playing this file? See [media help](/wiki/Help:Media "Help:Media").*

In the 2011 [Sveriges Television](/wiki/Sveriges_Television "Sveriges Television") documentary, Martin further commented: "The song Viva la Vida perhaps more than any of our songs went through about fifty-five different styles ... it always had the "dun dun dun" thing ... it sounded very heavy rock at one point, then it went completely acoustic." Guitarist [Jonny Buckland](/wiki/Jonny_Buckland "Jonny Buckland") quipped: "I think I wrote probably about fifteen riffs for that song, and none of them made it." Martin then explained: "We took a long time to work out how to do it, then one day we found that sort of church bell sound in the chorus, and then we finished it. It's really weird how you can have a song that's completely finished in terms of the lyrics and the melody and everything, but you're just missing one noise." Band manager [Phil Harvey](/wiki/Phil_Harvey_(band_manager) "Phil Harvey (band manager)") said: "I remember Chris hearing it and his face lit up and it was probably the happiest I've ever seen him in the recording studio. He just said 'That's it.'"[[11]](#cite_note-:7-11)

> "Viva la Vida" soars in with a grandiose instrumental arrangement and sweeping lyrics detailing the pain of being deposed from a lofty position. The big sound of the song constantly verges on becoming overblown, but Coldplay knows how to walk the tightrope perfectly. Bells and chimes and orchestral swells are all there on the chorus, but Chris Martin's voice still pierces through like a clarion call. Lyrically, the pain of the protagonist is clear, but the sweep of words about Jerusalem bells, Roman cavalry, and Saint Peter give "Viva la Vida" an air of intelligence rare in today's most popular pop songs.

— Bill Lamb, *About.com*.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

In another interview, Martin recalled: "We did quite a few different versions and went round the houses a bit and eventually settled on those treatments for it."[[15]](#cite_note-15) The soundscape of strings was arranged and performed solely by violinist and composer [Davide Rossi](/wiki/Davide_Rossi "Davide Rossi").[[16]](#cite_note-16)[[17]](#cite_note-17) In an interview, Berryman noted Rossi's arrangement created a full orchestral feeling and rectified an issue of flat dynamics in the song, without the need for an entire orchestra.[[18]](#cite_note-18) In a 2008 review, Geoff Boucher of the *[Los Angeles Times](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Times "Los Angeles Times")* described the strings as "[Beatles](/wiki/The_Beatles "The Beatles")-esque" and commented that the song has a "U2-style build", attributed to the producer [Brian Eno](/wiki/Brian_Eno "Brian Eno")'s previous work with the [Bono-led band](/wiki/U2 "U2"). Music critic Ann Powers, also of the *Los Angeles Times*, commented that Eno's presence has Coldplay making their "official leap towards greatness."[[19]](#cite_note-19)

## Lyrics and meaning

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Viva_la_Vida&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Lyrics and meaning")]

### Louis XVI and French Revolution

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Viva_la_Vida&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Louis XVI and French Revolution")]

The lyrics to "Viva la Vida" are narrated by a protagonist who says he "used to rule the world". One interpretation of the song is that it is a retelling of the [French Revolution](/wiki/French_Revolution "French Revolution") and spoken from the perspective of [King Louis XVI](/wiki/Louis_XVI "Louis XVI") before he is executed by [guillotine](/wiki/Guillotine "Guillotine"). The former ruler gave one final speech before his execution on 21 January 1793 at 10:22 am:[[20]](#cite_note-:3-20)[[21]](#cite_note-:4-21)

> "My People, I die innocent! Gentlemen, I am innocent of everything of which I am accused. I hope that my blood may cement the good fortune of the French."

The second verse may also be referring to Louis XVI's rise to power after the death of his grandfather [Louis XV](/wiki/Louis_XV "Louis XV") with the line: "Now the old king is dead, long live the king", which is almost identical to the [traditional proclamation](/wiki/The_king_is_dead,_long_live_the_king! "The king is dead, long live the king!").[[20]](#cite_note-:3-20)

The parallel to Louis XVI is also reinforced by the lines "And revolutionaries wait / For my head on a silver plate" in the third verse.[[4]](#cite_note-:8-4)

When asked about the song, bassist [Guy Berryman](/wiki/Guy_Berryman "Guy Berryman") said: "It's a story about a king who's lost his kingdom, and all the album's artwork is based on the idea of revolutionaries and [guerrillas](/wiki/Guerrillas "Guerrillas"). There's this slightly anti-authoritarian viewpoint that's crept into some of the lyrics and it's some of the pay-off between being surrounded by governments on one side, but also we're human beings with emotions and we're all going to die and the stupidity of what we have to put up with every day. Hence the album title."[[22]](#cite_note-22)

In the 2011 [Sveriges Television](/wiki/Sveriges_Television "Sveriges Television") documentary, Martin said "I always had an image of a king in his castle when everyone's banging down the door, feeling like 'OK, maybe I should change the way I do things' so there is an element of being able to change the world but only if you change the way you do things. So it's like someone who's made a lot of mistakes but feels optimistic that they can change it again." Half-jokingly, he added: "But really it's just a love song, because maybe it's a metaphorical about being in a relationship and making a mistake and trying to fix it. Could be. I don't know. Well I do know. Or do I?"[[11]](#cite_note-:7-11)

### Religious imagery

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Viva_la_Vida&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Religious imagery")]

[![Champion performing with the timpano and church bell used in the song](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/53/Will_Champion_-_Viva_la_Vida_tour.jpg/250px-Will_Champion_-_Viva_la_Vida_tour.jpg)](/wiki/File:Will_Champion_-_Viva_la_Vida_tour.jpg)

Champion with the [timpano](/wiki/Timpani "Timpani") and [church bell](/wiki/Church_bell "Church bell") used in the song

The song also heavily features historical and religious imagery in its lyrics – "pillars of salt and pillars of sand", "[Jerusalem](/wiki/Religious_significance_of_Jerusalem "Religious significance of Jerusalem") bells a-ringing", "[Roman cavalry](/wiki/Roman_cavalry "Roman cavalry") choirs", "[missionaries](/wiki/Missionary "Missionary")", and "[Saint Peter](/wiki/Saint_Peter "Saint Peter")".[[21]](#cite_note-:4-21)[[23]](#cite_note-23)[[24]](#cite_note-24) "Pillars of salt" is a reference to [Lot's wife](/wiki/Lot%27s_wife "Lot's wife") who was turned into one when she looked back at [Sodom](/wiki/Sodom_and_Gomorrah "Sodom and Gomorrah") during its destruction by God, while "pillars of sand" is a reference to [Matthew 7:24-27](/wiki/Matthew_7:24 "Matthew 7:24") – the parable Jesus tells about the man who built his house on sand.[[25]](#cite_note-:5-25)[[26]](#cite_note-26)

The lines "And revolutionaries wait / For my head on a silver plate" may also be a reference to [John the Baptist](/wiki/John_the_Baptist "John the Baptist"), whose head was requested on a silver platter by [Herod](/wiki/Herod_II "Herod II")'s stepdaughter [Salome](/wiki/Salome "Salome").[[25]](#cite_note-:5-25)

Columnist Mark Joseph commented in 2008 that the line "For some reason, I can't explain / I know Saint Peter won't call my name" sounded like a direct, melodic attack on the [Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church"),[[27]](#cite_note-27) while Martin himself explained the lyric in an interview with *[Q](/wiki/Q_(magazine) "Q (magazine)")* magazine: "It's about ... You're not on the list" to enter the [pearly gates](/wiki/Pearly_gates "Pearly gates").[[28]](#cite_note-28)

## Release and promotion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Viva_la_Vida&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Release and promotion")]

"Viva la Vida" was first made available on the [iTunes Store](/wiki/ITunes_Store "ITunes Store") on 7 May 2008.[[29]](#cite_note-29) The song was part of the pre-order marketing for *[Viva la Vida or Death and All His Friends](/wiki/Viva_la_Vida_or_Death_and_All_His_Friends "Viva la Vida or Death and All His Friends")*, which also included exclusive acoustic versions for "[Lost!](/wiki/Lost! "Lost!")" and "[Lovers in Japan](/wiki/Lovers_in_Japan "Lovers in Japan")".[[30]](#cite_note-30) On 25 May 2008, Coldplay released it for individual purchase globally.[[31]](#cite_note-31) However, since the track was already being given away as a free download to those who had pre-ordered the album, it remained ineligible for the [UK Singles Chart](/wiki/UK_Singles_Chart "UK Singles Chart") until 12 June 2008.[[32]](#cite_note-32) [Parlophone](/wiki/Parlophone "Parlophone") originally requested the band to not release "Viva la Vida", arguing it "did not sound like Coldplay", but drummer [Will Champion](/wiki/Will_Champion "Will Champion") insisted on the release.[[33]](#cite_note-33)

It became the best-selling song of 2008 on iTunes and was used as part of [Apple](/wiki/Apple_Inc. "Apple Inc.")'s [iPod + iTunes advertisement campaign](/wiki/IPod_advertising "IPod advertising").[[34]](#cite_note-ew_review-34)[[35]](#cite_note-35)[[36]](#cite_note-36) Coldplay first performed "Viva la Vida" live at the [MTV Movie Awards](/wiki/2008_MTV_Movie_Awards "2008 MTV Movie Awards").[[37]](#cite_note-37)[[38]](#cite_note-38)

## Music videos

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Viva_la_Vida&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Music videos")]

[![Chris Martin dressed as a king in the Anton Corbijn video](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/0/02/Coldplay_-_Viva_La_Vida_Official_Alternate_Screenshot.jpg/250px-Coldplay_-_Viva_La_Vida_Official_Alternate_Screenshot.jpg)](/wiki/File:Coldplay_-_Viva_La_Vida_Official_Alternate_Screenshot.jpg)

Chris Martin as the king in the [Anton Corbijn](/wiki/Anton_Corbijn "Anton Corbijn") video

The official music video for "Viva la Vida" was directed by [Hype Williams](/wiki/Hype_Williams "Hype Williams") and premiered at Coldplay's official website on 1 August 2008,[[39]](#cite_note-39)[[40]](#cite_note-40) and was later released on 4 August 2008 on [YouTube](/wiki/YouTube "YouTube").[[41]](#cite_note-41) The video depicts the band performing against a blurry, warped version of [Eugène Delacroix](/wiki/Eug%C3%A8ne_Delacroix "Eugène Delacroix")'s painting *[Liberty Leading the People](/wiki/Liberty_Leading_the_People "Liberty Leading the People")*, ending with the band members crumbling into rose petals.[[42]](#cite_note-42)[[43]](#cite_note-:6-43)

A second, alternate video was shot in [The Hague](/wiki/The_Hague "The Hague"), the Netherlands, directed by [Anton Corbijn](/wiki/Anton_Corbijn "Anton Corbijn") and released alongside the first.[[44]](#cite_note-44)[[45]](#cite_note-45) This version is a tribute to Corbijn's video for [Depeche Mode](/wiki/Depeche_Mode "Depeche Mode")'s "[Enjoy the Silence](/wiki/Enjoy_the_Silence "Enjoy the Silence")" and portrays Chris Martin as the king from whose perspective the song is sung.[[46]](#cite_note-46) During the video, he carries Delacroix's painting under his arm, while [Dave Gahan](/wiki/Dave_Gahan "Dave Gahan") carries a deck chair under his arm in the "Enjoy the Silence" video. Both are dressed in robes and wearing a crown.[[43]](#cite_note-:6-43)

## Critical reception

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Viva_la_Vida&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Critical reception")]

[![Champion plays the drums for "Viva la Vida" during the Viva la Vida Tour in 2009.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cc/Will_Champion_-_Viva_la_Vida.jpg/250px-Will_Champion_-_Viva_la_Vida.jpg)](/wiki/File:Will_Champion_-_Viva_la_Vida.jpg)

Champion performing "Viva la Vida" during the [Viva la Vida Tour](/wiki/Viva_la_Vida_Tour "Viva la Vida Tour") in 2009.

"Viva la Vida" received widespread critical acclaim.[[47]](#cite_note-47)[[48]](#cite_note-48) In the *[Entertainment Weekly](/wiki/Entertainment_Weekly "Entertainment Weekly")* review of the album, critic Chris Willman wrote: "Take the title track ... on which [Martin] imagines himself as a paranoid monarch. 'Who would ever want to be king?' Martin asks. 'Revolutionaries wait/For my head on a silver plate!' The confident majesty of the music, however, belies how he and his bandmates have invigorated their rock-lite reign."[[34]](#cite_note-ew_review-34) Josh Hathaway from *[The Plain Dealer](/wiki/The_Plain_Dealer "The Plain Dealer")* noted "Viva la Vida" as the "catchiest" song on the album.[[49]](#cite_note-49) Chris Jones of the [BBC](/wiki/BBC "BBC") noted: "The string/brass mutations that bolster a track like 'Viva la Vida' ... conjure tunes so sweetly melancholy."[[50]](#cite_note-50) In the [IGN](/wiki/IGN "IGN") review, critic Chad Grischow wrote, "It is their one and only foray into unabashed orchestral pop, but the punchy strut of the strings and fantastic marching vocals make it far too charming and lively to dislike, and even harder not to love."[[51]](#cite_note-51)

"Viva la Vida" was nominated for "[Record of the Year](/wiki/Grammy_Award_for_Record_of_the_Year "Grammy Award for Record of the Year")", and won "[Song of the Year](/wiki/Grammy_Award_for_Song_of_the_Year "Grammy Award for Song of the Year")", and "[Best Pop Performance by a Duo or a Group](/wiki/Grammy_Award_for_Best_Pop_Performance_by_a_Duo_or_Group_with_Vocal "Grammy Award for Best Pop Performance by a Duo or Group with Vocal")" at the [2009 Grammy Awards](/wiki/2009_Grammy_Awards "2009 Grammy Awards").[[52]](#cite_note-52)[[53]](#cite_note-53) This song also won the [Ivor Novello Award](/wiki/Ivor_Novello_Award "Ivor Novello Award") for "Best Selling British Single".[[54]](#cite_note-54) The song was included in *[Rolling Stone](/wiki/Rolling_Stone "Rolling Stone")*'s annual "100 Best Songs" of 2008 at number nine;[[55]](#cite_note-55) it was also voted number two on *Rolling Stone*'s Readers' Rock List: Best Songs of 2008.[[56]](#cite_note-56) "Viva la Vida" was also listed at number five on *Blender*'s 1001 Downloads: The Top 144 Songs of 2008,[[57]](#cite_note-57) as well in the number eight position on *Village Voice*'s Pazz and Jop list.[[58]](#cite_note-58) In 2019, *[Billboard](/wiki/Billboard_(magazine) "Billboard (magazine)")* ranked the song number seven on their list of the 50 greatest Coldplay songs.[[59]](#cite_note-59) In 2021, *[American Songwriter](/wiki/American_Songwriter "American Songwriter")* placed the song at number one on their own version of the ranking.[[60]](#cite_note-60)

## Chart performance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Viva_la_Vida&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Chart performance")]

Driven by digital sales,[[79]](#cite_note-CaulfieldHeaded-79) "Viva la Vida" became Coldplay's first song to top the [*Billboard* Hot 100](/wiki/Billboard_Hot_100 "Billboard Hot 100") and their second to enter the Top 10, following "[Speed of Sound](/wiki/Speed_of_Sound_(song) "Speed of Sound (song)")".[[80]](#cite_note-CohenDrive-80) It was also the first single from a British act to reach number one in the United Kingdom and United States simultaneously since "[Maggie May](/wiki/Maggie_May "Maggie May")" by [Rod Stewart](/wiki/Rod_Stewart "Rod Stewart") in 1971.[[81]](#cite_note-81) The track later peaked at number eight on the [Hot 100 Airplay](/wiki/Radio_Songs_(chart) "Radio Songs (chart)"), giving the group their highest-charting release in American radio.[[82]](#cite_note-82) "Viva la Vida" topped *Billboard*'s [Modern Rock](/wiki/Alternative_Airplay "Alternative Airplay") and [Hot Adult Top 40](/wiki/Adult_Pop_Airplay "Adult Pop Airplay") charts as well,[[83]](#cite_note-chart_history-83) becoming the first single in [Capitol](/wiki/Capitol_Records "Capitol Records") history to achieve the feat on the latter.[[84]](#cite_note-84)

Despite being disqualified from British rankings at first due to album pre-order restrictions on [iTunes](/wiki/ITunes "ITunes"), the song eventually debuted atop the [UK Singles Chart](/wiki/UK_singles_chart "UK singles chart"), making it Coldplay's first track to do so.[[85]](#cite_note-85) In June 2024, [PPL](/wiki/Phonographic_Performance_Limited "Phonographic Performance Limited") ranked "Viva la Vida" as the band's most played song across radio and television in the United Kingdom, averaging 27 reproductions a day since release.[[86]](#cite_note-86) A year later, the track was placed at number 159 on the list of most streamed songs of all time on [Apple Music](/wiki/Apple_Music "Apple Music").[[87]](#cite_note-87)

In Canada, the single made a "Hot Shot Debut" at number four on the [Canadian Hot 100](/wiki/Canadian_Hot_100 "Canadian Hot 100") on the 24 May issue, making it Coldplay's highest debut there.[[88]](#cite_note-88) It is also one of Coldplay's three highest-charting singles in [Australia](/wiki/Australia "Australia"), reaching number two (both "[A Sky Full of Stars](/wiki/A_Sky_Full_of_Stars "A Sky Full of Stars")" and "[Something Just Like This](/wiki/Something_Just_Like_This "Something Just Like This")" matched this peak in 2014 and 2017, respectively).[[89]](#cite_note-89) In the week starting 27 November 2023, the single once again returned to the same charts, entering at number 28.[[90]](#cite_note-ARIA2023-90) In [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand "New Zealand"), the song was less successful, peaking at number 16; however, the song stayed in the top 40 for 27 non-consecutive weeks.[[91]](#cite_note-91) In Spain, the single peaked at number two and had strong airplay on the Spanish radio stations.[[92]](#cite_note-92)

## Sporting usage

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Viva_la_Vida&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Sporting usage")]

[![Martin performing "Viva la Vida" during the band's 2008 Viva la Vida Tour](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/61/Chris-Martin-BBC-live.jpg/250px-Chris-Martin-BBC-live.jpg)](/wiki/File:Chris-Martin-BBC-live.jpg)

Martin performing "Viva la Vida" during the band's 2008 [Viva la Vida Tour](/wiki/Viva_la_Vida_Tour "Viva la Vida Tour")

"Viva la Vida" is used as the goal celebration song for the German football club [Hannover 96](/wiki/Hannover_96 "Hannover 96").[[93]](#cite_note-93)
The song also became the anthem of Spanish football club [FC Barcelona](/wiki/FC_Barcelona "FC Barcelona") starting from the [2008–09 season](/wiki/2008%E2%80%9309_FC_Barcelona_season "2008–09 FC Barcelona season"),[[94]](#cite_note-94) who won all six competitions they could possibly be involved in.[[95]](#cite_note-95) The song was selected by the manager of the club, [Pep Guardiola](/wiki/Pep_Guardiola "Pep Guardiola") – himself a Coldplay fan – to help motivate and encourage his team.[[96]](#cite_note-96)[[97]](#cite_note-97)[[98]](#cite_note-98) [UEFA](/wiki/UEFA "UEFA") used "Viva la Vida" as the goal celebration song for the [2012 UEFA Champions League Final](/wiki/2012_UEFA_Champions_League_Final "2012 UEFA Champions League Final")[[99]](#cite_note-99) and the [2017–18 UEFA Europa League Final](/wiki/2017%E2%80%9318_UEFA_Europa_League "2017–18 UEFA Europa League").[[100]](#cite_note-100)

The song was also performed by the band during the closing ceremony of the London 2012 Paralympic Games. The song was used to close the event and therefore end the ceremony.

## Plagiarism allegations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Viva_la_Vida&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Plagiarism allegations")]

Coldplay were first accused of [plagiarism](/wiki/Plagiarism "Plagiarism") for the melody of "Viva la Vida" by American band Creaky Boards.[[101]](#cite_note-101) Singer Andrew Hoepfner claimed that Martin had heard them playing their piece "The Songs I Didn't Write" at a live show in October 2007.[[102]](#cite_note-NMECopied-102) They also published a video comparing sections of both tracks.[[102]](#cite_note-NMECopied-102) A spokesperson for Coldplay stated that Martin was working at [AIR Studios](/wiki/Associated_Independent_Recording "Associated Independent Recording") in London at the time.[[103]](#cite_note-Telegraph-103) Additionally, a [demo](/wiki/Demo_(music) "Demo (music)") of "Viva la Vida" was recorded in March 2007.[[102]](#cite_note-NMECopied-102) Creaky Boards later retracted the accusations and speculated that both songs may have been inspired by the video game *[The Legend of Zelda](/wiki/The_Legend_of_Zelda_(video_game) "The Legend of Zelda (video game)")*.[[103]](#cite_note-Telegraph-103)

In December 2008, American guitarist [Joe Satriani](/wiki/Joe_Satriani "Joe Satriani") filed a copyright infringement lawsuit against Coldplay in [Los Angeles](/wiki/Los_Angeles "Los Angeles").[[104]](#cite_note-104) It declared that "Viva la Vida" incorporates "substantial, original portions" of his composition "If I Could Fly", from *[Is There Love in Space?](/wiki/Is_There_Love_in_Space%3F "Is There Love in Space?")* (2004).[[105]](#cite_note-105) The band denied the allegation, saying the similarities were entirely coincidental.[[106]](#cite_note-106) The case was later dismissed by the [California Central District Court](/wiki/United_States_District_Court_for_the_Central_District_of_California "United States District Court for the Central District of California"),[[107]](#cite_note-107) with each party paying their own costs.[[108]](#cite_note-108)

In May 2009, British musician [Cat Stevens](/wiki/Cat_Stevens "Cat Stevens") stated that "Viva la Vida" is similar to "[Foreigner Suite](/wiki/Foreigner_(Cat_Stevens_album) "Foreigner (Cat Stevens album)")" (1973), comparing them after talking with his son about it.[[109]](#cite_note-:0-109) Stevens informed that any legal action he intended to take depended "on how well Satriani does".[[109]](#cite_note-:0-109) In June 2009, he claimed that "They did copy my song but I don't think they did it on purpose [...] I don't want them to think I am angry with them. I'd love to sit down and have a cup of tea with them and let them know it's okay".[[110]](#cite_note-110)

In a documentary for [Sveriges Television](/wiki/Sveriges_Television "Sveriges Television") (2011), American music professor Dr. Lawrence Ferrara showed that the melody structures of "Viva la Vida", "If I Could Fly" and "Foreigner Suite" were all similar to the track "Se Tu M'Ami", which has its authorship attributed to Italian composers [Alessandro Parisotti](/wiki/Alessandro_Parisotti "Alessandro Parisotti") and [Giovanni Battista Pergolesi](/wiki/Giovanni_Battista_Pergolesi "Giovanni Battista Pergolesi").[[111]](#cite_note-111)[[112]](#cite_note-:1-112) The latter died in 1736.[[113]](#cite_note-113) "Obviously this is a work that we would call in the 'public domain'", Ferrara concluded.[[112]](#cite_note-:1-112) Discussing the accusations, drummer Champion stated that "We're confident we haven't done anything wrong" and "for some reason, God only knows why, the successful songs seem to be the ones that are accused of being stolen".[[114]](#cite_note-114) Martin later revealed that the plagiarism allegations were inspiring to him: "If everyone's trying to take away our best song, then we'd better write 25 better ones".[[115]](#cite_note-115)

## Legacy and popular culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Viva_la_Vida&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Legacy and popular culture")]

"Viva la Vida" has made many media appearances, including being featured throughout the episode ["A Person of Interest"](/wiki/List_of_Medium_episodes#Season_5:_2009 "List of Medium episodes") from the [paranormal](/wiki/Paranormal "Paranormal") drama *[Medium](/wiki/Medium_(TV_Series) "Medium (TV Series)")*, as a song played on the radio in the episode "We're Not in Kansas Anymore" from the teen drama *[90210](/wiki/90210_(TV_series) "90210 (TV series)")*,[[116]](#cite_note-116) and on the international soundtrack to the Brazilian soap opera *[A Favorita](/wiki/A_Favorita "A Favorita")* which helped push the track up the charts in Brazil, where the telenovela had been shown.[[117]](#cite_note-Som_Livre-117)

It was also used in "[Million Dollar Maybe](/wiki/Million_Dollar_Maybe "Million Dollar Maybe")", an episode of *[The Simpsons](/wiki/The_Simpsons "The Simpsons")*.[[118]](#cite_note-118) The song was covered in the [seventh series](/wiki/The_X_Factor_(UK_series_7) "The X Factor (UK series 7)") of *[The X Factor](/wiki/The_X_Factor_(UK) "The X Factor (UK)")* by the boyband [One Direction](/wiki/One_Direction "One Direction") in 2010.[[119]](#cite_note-119) In 2024, the season 2 finale of [Pachinko](/wiki/Pachinko_(TV_series) "Pachinko (TV series)") ends with a cover by [Rosé](/wiki/Ros%C3%A9_(singer) "Rosé (singer)") of the Korean pop group [Blackpink](/wiki/Blackpink "Blackpink").[[120]](#cite_note-120) It has also been sampled in several other songs, including [Flo Rida](/wiki/Flo_Rida "Flo Rida")'s "[Be on You](/wiki/Be_on_You "Be on You")",[[121]](#cite_note-121) and [Drake](/wiki/Drake_(musician) "Drake (musician)")'s "Congratulations" from his [So Far Gone](/wiki/So_Far_Gone_(mixtape) "So Far Gone (mixtape)") mixtape.[[21]](#cite_note-:4-21)

The song was performed by the band at the [One Love Manchester](/wiki/One_Love_Manchester "One Love Manchester") benefit concert, which took place on June 4th 2017 to help support and raise money for the families and friends, who lost loved ones during the [Manchester Arena bombing](/wiki/Manchester_Arena_bombing "Manchester Arena bombing") which occurred on May 22nd 2017 in [Manchester](/wiki/Manchester "Manchester"), [England](/wiki/England "England"), shortly after [Ariana Grande](/wiki/Ariana_Grande "Ariana Grande") concluded her [Dangerous Woman Tour](/wiki/Dangerous_Woman_Tour "Dangerous Woman Tour") show in Manchester. [[122]](#cite_note-122)

“Viva la Vida” is featured within the popular dancing video game franchise: [Just Dance](/wiki/Just_Dance_(video_game_series) "Just Dance (video game series)"). The song is included in the main track list of the [2026 Edition](/wiki/Just_Dance_2026_Edition "Just Dance 2026 Edition") of the game.[[123]](#cite_note-123)

It is one of the most-streamed songs on Spotify with over 3.1 billion plays.[[124]](#cite_note-124)

## Covers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Viva_la_Vida&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Covers")]

See also: [List of cover versions of Coldplay songs](/wiki/List_of_cover_versions_of_Coldplay_songs "List of cover versions of Coldplay songs")

In 2009, [Solange Knowles](/wiki/Solange_Knowles "Solange Knowles") covered "Viva la Vida".[[132]](#cite_note-132) [Lady Gaga](/wiki/Lady_Gaga "Lady Gaga") also covered it for [BBC Radio 1](/wiki/BBC_Radio_1 "BBC Radio 1").[[133]](#cite_note-133) Swedish singer [Darin](/wiki/Darin_(singer) "Darin (singer)")'s cover of the song reached number 1 on 30 October 2009 on the [Swedish Singles Chart](/wiki/Sverigetopplistan "Sverigetopplistan")[[134]](#cite_note-134) and was certified Gold by the [GLF](/wiki/Swedish_Recording_Industry_Association "Swedish Recording Industry Association") in 2010.[[135]](#cite_note-135) The song appears on Darin's 2010 album *[Lovekiller](/wiki/Lovekiller_(album) "Lovekiller (album)")*. In 2010, it was yet again included as the B-side of Darin's "[You're Out of My Life](/wiki/You%27re_Out_of_My_Life_(Darin_song) "You're Out of My Life (Darin song)")" single, reaching No. 3 in the [Swedish Singles Chart](/wiki/Sverigetopplistan "Sverigetopplistan"). Coldplay's original single had already charted in Sweden for a total of 49 weeks between 15 May 2008 and 23 October 2009, peaking at number seven.[[136]](#cite_note-136) [Weezer](/wiki/Weezer "Weezer") recorded a cover of the song for the deluxe version of their 2010 album [Hurley](/wiki/Hurley_(album) "Hurley (album)").[[137]](#cite_note-137) In 2012, YouTuber and musician [CaptainSparklez](/wiki/CaptainSparklez "CaptainSparklez") released a [Minecraft](/wiki/Minecraft "Minecraft") parody of the song called "Fallen Kingdom", which adapts the song's original lyrics to fit a Minecraft narrative of a king losing his kingdom to mobs.[[138]](#cite_note-138)
